coredata.dc:title: UiO-66-NH2 initiated cascade reaction: Constructing a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for ultrasensitive detection of nerve agent simulant
coredata.dc:creator: Lei, You
Gao, Nan
Huang, Pengcheng
Wu, Fang-Ying
coredata.dc:description:
Background Highly toxic organophosphorus nerve agents often exist in the form of gas in the environment and can damage human neuroregulatory system by inhibiting the activity of acetylcholinesterase (AChE). However, fluorescent probes based on small organic molecules bring a secondary burden to environment, and their sensitivity and specificity for sarin simulant diethyl chlorophosphate (DCP) detection are unsatisfactory. Nanozyme cascade systems with signal amplification can be used for highly sensitive identification of analytes, but are rarely used in ratiometric analysis of DCP. Combination of enzyme cascades and ratiometric fluorescence ensures the accuracy and sensitivity of the output signal.
Results We prepared a self-assembled nanohybrid (Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2) by metal-organic framework material and gold nanoclusters. On the one hand, UiO-66-NH2 with enzyme-like activity was used to hydrolyze DCP into diethyl phosphate (DEP) and chloridion (Cl−). Cl− hindered aggregation-induced enhanced emission (AIEE) of AuNCs by binding with Ag+ and decreased the fluorescence of AuNCs. On the other hand, ligand metal charge transfer effect (LMCT) of UiO-66-NH2 was blocked by DCP to enhance the fluorescence of UiO-66-NH2. Combining ratiometric analysis and nanozyme cascade reaction, an ultra-sensitive fluorescence sensor for detecting DCP was constructed, and ensured the accuracy of experimental results. In addition, Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 was embedded into the agarose hydrogel substrate, the resulting agarose hydrogel film allowed quantitative assessment of DCP vapor and high sensitivity was demonstrated (detection limit as low as 1.02 ppb).
Significance A strategy combining enzyme cascade with ratiometric fluorescence was proposed, which improved the accuracy and sensitivity of the analysis results. The soft-solid platform based on agarose hydrogel film was constructed to realize the quantitative monitoring of sarin simulant gas. The LOD value obtained in this work is much lower than the immediately life-threatening or health threatening concentration of sarin.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoclusters
Diethyl chlorophosphate
Gas detection
Hazardous vapor
Nerve agents

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for fluorescence detection of DCP in aqueous solution
Preparation the composite agarose hydrogels of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2
Detection DCP vapors with composite hydrogels
Analytical performance of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 for DCP in water
Possible principle of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 for DCP detection
DCP vapor detection using Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 hydrogel sheet

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( NSFC ) (No. 22164013 and No. 21864018 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of AuNCs. Inset: Histogram of particle size distribution of AuNCs, (b) Fluorescence spectra of AuNCs and Ag–AuNCs. Inset: Fluorescence photograph of AuNCs and Ag–AuNCs, (c) TEM image of Ag–AuNCs, (d) Fluorescence lifetime curves of AuNCs and Ag–AuNCs, (e) High-resolution XPS analysis of Au 4f, (f) High-resolution XPS analysis of Ag 3d.

(a) TEM image of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2, (b) Magnification of TEM image of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2, (c) Elemental mapping of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2, (d) Zeta potential of Ag–AuNCs, UiO-66-NH2 and Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2, (e) FT-IR spectrogram of Ag–AuNCs, UiO-66-NH2 and Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2, (f) XPS spectra of Ag–AuNCs, UiO-66-NH2 and Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2.

(a) Fluorescence spectrum of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 after addition of DCP (0–180 μM). Inset: Fluorescence photographs of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 with different DCP concentrations (0–180 μM), (b) Plot of F465 nm/F650 nm versus DCP concentration, (c) Fluorescence spectra of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 after reaction with different distractors (green line: Na2HPO4; red line: DCP; blue line: TEA; sky blue line: NaH2PO4; yellow line: Na3PO4; pink line: MCE). (d) Fluorescence response (F465 nm/F650 nm) of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 to DCP and other distractors (60 μM). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Possible sensing mechanism between Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 and DCP, (b) Emission spectrum of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 reacting with DCP, DEP and Cl−, (c) TEM image of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 in the absence of DCP, (d) TEM image of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 in the presence of DCP.

(a) Schematic illustration of DCP vapor detection, (b) Fluorescence colorimetric card for quantitative detection of DCP vapor by agarose hydrogel loaded with Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2, (c) G + B–R values of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 hydrogel sheets exposed to different doses of DCP vapor, (d) Linear relationship between G + B–R values and DCP vapor concentrations, (e) G + B–R values of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 hydrogel sheets when exposed to DCP vapor and other vapors.
Construction of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 nanohybrid and proportional fluorescence sensing strategy of DCP.

head.ce:title: UiO-66-NH2 initiated cascade reaction: Constructing a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for ultrasensitive detection of nerve agent simulant

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly toxic organophosphorus nerve agents often exist in the form of gas in the environment and can damage human neuroregulatory system by inhibiting the activity of acetylcholinesterase (AChE). However, fluorescent probes based on small organic molecules bring a secondary burden to environment, and their sensitivity and specificity for sarin simulant diethyl chlorophosphate (DCP) detection are unsatisfactory. Nanozyme cascade systems with signal amplification can be used for highly sensitive identification of analytes, but are rarely used in ratiometric analysis of DCP. Combination of enzyme cascades and ratiometric fluorescence ensures the accuracy and sensitivity of the output signal.

We prepared a self-assembled nanohybrid (Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2) by metal-organic framework material and gold nanoclusters. On the one hand, UiO-66-NH2 with enzyme-like activity was used to hydrolyze DCP into diethyl phosphate (DEP) and chloridion (Cl−). Cl− hindered aggregation-induced enhanced emission (AIEE) of AuNCs by binding with Ag+ and decreased the fluorescence of AuNCs. On the other hand, ligand metal charge transfer effect (LMCT) of UiO-66-NH2 was blocked by DCP to enhance the fluorescence of UiO-66-NH2. Combining ratiometric analysis and nanozyme cascade reaction, an ultra-sensitive fluorescence sensor for detecting DCP was constructed, and ensured the accuracy of experimental results. In addition, Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 was embedded into the agarose hydrogel substrate, the resulting agarose hydrogel film allowed quantitative assessment of DCP vapor and high sensitivity was demonstrated (detection limit as low as 1.02 ppb).

A strategy combining enzyme cascade with ratiometric fluorescence was proposed, which improved the accuracy and sensitivity of the analysis results. The soft-solid platform based on agarose hydrogel film was constructed to realize the quantitative monitoring of sarin simulant gas. The LOD value obtained in this work is much lower than the immediately life-threatening or health threatening concentration of sarin.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nerve agents containing phosphate or phosphate ester groups, which mainly inhibit the activity of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) by irreversibly reacting with serine hydroxyl group of AChE, thereby destroying neuroregulatory system of human body and leading to death [1–3]. So far, a multiplicity of methods had been developed to meet the sensing of nerve agents, including gas/liquid chromatography, surface acoustic waves, enhanced Raman spectroscopy and enzyme biosensing technology [4–8]. Although these methods have achieved good results, their further application is hindered by the disadvantages such as complex operation, time consuming, and the need for large instruments.

Fluorescent probe-based detection technology has been widely used for its advantages of naked eye observation, low cost, and real-time response [9–11]. In particular, ratiometric fluorescent sensors can effectively overcome the changes in absolute fluorescence intensity caused by factors uncorrelated to analytes, avoid false positive or false negative outcomes, and ensure the accuracy and reliability of experimental results [12,13]. Enzymatic cascades with signal amplification efficacy can significantly improve analyte sensitivity [14]. However, the application of sensors based on cascade catalytic systems in the ratiometric fluorescence detection of nerve agents has been rarely reported. Nanozymes have similar catalytic activities to biological enzymes, and have been used in various fields owing to their low synthesis cost, strong resistance to environmental interference, and excellent biocompatibility [15,16]. Among them, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) emerged as one of the most promising nanozymes due to their high crystallinity, adjustable pore structure, and large number of catalytic active sites [17,18]. In particular, the amino functionalized UiO-66 (UiO-66-NH2) not only contains Lewis acidic Zr6O4(OH)4 cluster that simulate the Zn–O–Zn catalytic active site in phosphotriesterase, but also acts as a proximate base to promote the regeneration of the active Zr site and become an effective catalyst for nerve agent detoxification [19]. Therefore, UiO-66-NH2 can be used as a nanozyme for the fluorescence sensing platform of the cascade catalytic system.

In our previous work, we found that UiO-66-NH2 could be used as an optical sensor for quantitative analysis of sarin mimic diethyl chlorophosphate (DCP) [20]. However, the fluorescent probe based on a single emission is difficult to resist the interference of various factors in the environment, which may produce wrong signals and bring deviation to experimental results [21,22]. At the same time, compared with the change of fluorescence color, the change of fluorescence brightness is not so convenient for the visual monitoring of DCP. Gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) with the superiority of large Stokes shift, stable optical properties, and long lifetime, which have been reported in various fields, such as cancer diagnosis, treatment, biological imaging, biochemical sensing [23–26]. In particular, the design of Ag+ modified AuNCs enhanced fluorescence intensity is attractive and improved the sensitivity and practicality of AuNCs in sensing applications [27,28]. Therefore, the combination of MOF-based nanozymes and Ag–AuNCs to construct a nanozyme cascade catalytic system offers a novel idea for the specific sensing of DCP.

Herein, a cascade reaction system mediated by nerve agent hydrolases was proposed and successfully used for the sensitive detection of DCP. As shown in Scheme 1 , UiO-66-NH2 was obtained through solvothermal method, fluorescence of ligand BDC-NH2 was quenched due to the ligand metal charge transfer effect (LMCT). DCP could coordinate with the Zr–O node in UiO-66-NH2, greatly hinder LMCT effect and enhance the fluorescence of BDC-NH2. Notably, UiO-66-NH2 acts as a nanozyme to hydrolyze DCP to diethyl phosphate (DEP) and chloridion (Cl−). Subsequently, Cl− interacted with Ag+ in Ag–AuNCs, assisting Ag+ to dissociate from AuNCs and hindering the aggregation-induced enhanced emission (AIEE) of AuNCs triggered by Ag+, thus effectively decreasing the fluorescence of Ag–AuNCs. The results showed that combination of enzyme cascade and ratiometric analysis greatly improved the signal response. At the same time, the emission peaks of UiO-66-NH2 and Ag–AuNCs are separated greatly to avoid the overlap of fluorescence emission spectra, and the ratiometric signal avoided potential interference to experimental results. Meanwhile, introduction of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 into agarose hydrogel, a portable kit combined with a smart phone was designed to realize the quantitative detection of low concentration DCP vapor, which provided ideas for the visual detection and tracking of dangerous gases in the field.
Drugs and instruments involved in the experiments were offered in the supporting information.

Firstly, AuNCs were synthesized with minor modifications in accordance with the previously reported method [29]. In brief, 40 mL HAuCl4 (10 mM) was injected to 154 mL deionized water. Then, 6 mL glutathione (100 mM) was added and mixed well and transferred the mixture to an oil bath at 70 °C for 24 h to obtain a yellow liquid. Next, AuNCs solution was dialyzed in deionized water for 48 h with a dialyzer (MWCO 3500 Da), and changed the deionized water frequently. Finally, AuNCs solution was thinned to 250 mL with deionized water and preserved in darkness at 4 °C.

Following that, Ag–AuNCs was synthesized with reference method [30]. 20 mL Ag+ (0.1 mg mL−1) was injected into the 50 mL solution of AuNCs synthesized above and mixed evenly, Ag–AuNCs generated by ultrasonic reaction for 10 min at 30 °C. Then the Ag–AuNCs solution was dialyzed with a dialyzer (MWCO 3500 Da) for 48 h, and the deionized water was changed frequently to remove unreacted Ag+. Lastly, Ag–AuNCs solution was stored at 4 °C in the dark.

UiO-66-NH2 was synthesized in accordance with previous report with minor modifications [31]. In brief, 0.252 g ZrCl4 and 0.272 g BDC-NH2 were transferred to a mingled solution of 30 mL DMF and 2 mL concentrated HCl and completely dissolved by sonication. The mixture was allowed to react for 18 h in an oven at 120 °C. Then, the resulting product was centrifuged at 1200 rpm for 5 min to obtain pale yellow solid. Subsequently, the above solid was washed several times with DMF and ethanol, respectively, and activated overnight at 80 °C in vacuum.

Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 was prepared following the following steps. Specifically, 80 μL Ag–AuNCs solution and 40 μL UiO-66-NH2 (1 mg mL−1) were mixed with 880 μL aqueous solution, and the mixture was equilibrated at 30 °C for 10 min to obtain Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 nanohybrid and perform the following series of experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for fluorescence detection of DCP in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture of 10 μL DCP (10 mM) and 990 μL Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 was obtained and reacted for 5 min at 25 °C. The fluorescence spectra were gained under 255 nm excitation. After that, fluorescence intensity ratios (F465 nm/F650 nm) were worked out.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation the composite agarose hydrogels of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2
ce:section.ce:para: Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 composite agarose hydrogels were prepared via the following steps. Firstly, 0.3 mL UiO-66-NH2 (1 mg mL−1), 0.5 mL Ag–AuNCs and 1.2 mL pure water were mixed in a 25 mL beaker and stably equilibrated at room temperature for 10 min. Subsequently, 0.05 g agarose was added and heated until utterly melted, then cooled to gel in a Petri dish. The agarose hydrogel was next embossed into hydrogel sheets of the same size using the tube cap of a 5 mL centrifuge tube for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection DCP vapors with composite hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: 2 mL DCP stock solution was putted in a 50 mL centrifuge tube and left for 48 h at 25 °C to get saturated vapor (132 ppm) [32]. First, the hydrogel sheet was put into another 50 mL centrifuge tube, and then saturated steam was injected into it with a microsyringe. Hydrogel sheet was fetched after reacted 1.5 min and took photos with a smartphone in a dark room under a 254 nm UV lamp (keep the position and angle of the smartphone and the hydrogel sheets still), then the RGB values of collected photos were calculated by Image J software.

AuNCs with light yellow liquid were prepared by one-pot reaction at 70 °C for 24 h with glutathione (GSH) as reducing and protecting agent. Firstly, the morphology of AuNCs was characterized using electron microscope (TEM), as illustrated in Fig. 1 a, AuNCs were well dispersed and had a quasi-spherical structure with an average size of approximately 2.34 nm. Next, AuNCs were modified with Ag+, and the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs at 650 nm was subsequently enhanced with the increase of Ag+ concentration in solution, and an equilibrium was reached at a volume ratio of Ag+ to AuNCs of 2:5 (Fig. S1). In addition, fluorescence spectra in Fig. 1b indicated that Ag+ caused the maximum emission peak of AuNCs to shift to a certain extent (from 625 nm to 650 nm), and the fluorescence color of AuNCs altered from dark red to brilliant red under a 254 nm UV lamp. This may be because the presence of Ag+ triggered the AIEE phenomenon of AuNCs [33]. TEM image in Fig. 1c can be observed that AuNCs changed from a dispersed state to an aggregated state in presence of Ag+, indicating the formation of Ag–AuNCs complexes. Fig. 1d showed that the fluorescence lifetime of AuNCs increased from 6.94 μs to 7.98 μs, and the relative fluorescence quantum yield increased from 2.53% to 7.92% after the introduction of Ag+, which further suggested that the increase of AuNCs emission intensity was caused by the AIEE phenomenon of AuNCs induced by Ag+. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis in Fig. 1e showed that most of the Au in Ag–AuNCs was in the reduced state (Au0) and a small part was in the oxidized state (Au+), and that these small amounts of Au+ contributed to the stability of AuNCs. Fig. 1f showed that most of Ag+ was reduced to Ag0, and we guessed that Ag + oxidize Au0 to Au + [34].

Next, TEM was used to study the appearance of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2. Unlike smooth, spherical structure of UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. S2), the outside of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 was coated with Ag–AuNCs (Fig. 2 a and b), which exhibited a rougher structure. The corresponding EDS element mapping image (Fig. 2c) revealed that C, N, O, Zr, Au, and Ag were evenly allocated in Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 with corresponding weight percentages of 59.30%, 3.91%, 19.17%, 15.53%, 1.77% and 0.31%, respectively, confirming the successful synthesis of the material. Furthermore, Zeta potential, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) were applied to systematically research the autonomous assembly process of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 nanohybrid. Firstly, the Zeta potential data were collected. As demonstrated in Fig. 2d, the Ag–AuNCs were negatively charged because of the deprotonation of GSH carboxyl groups on the surface of Ag–AuNCs, and the Zeta potential was −22.16 mV. Simultaneously, the protonation of quantities of amino groups on the surface of UiO-66-NH2 resulted in its positive charge with a Zeta potential of +15.07 mV. Reacted with Ag–AuNCs, Zeta potential of UiO-66-NH2 decreased from +15.07 mV to −10.89 mV, indicating that there was an electrostatic interaction between Ag–AuNCs and UiO-66-NH2 [35].

In addition, Fig. 2e described the FT-IR of Ag–AuNCs, UiO-66-NH2 and Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2. The appearance of vibrational stretching bands at 1648 cm−1 and 1395 cm−1 for Ag–AuNCs, corresponding to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of carboxyl groups, proved the existence of carboxyl groups on Ag–AuNCs [36]. The characteristic peaks of UiO-66-NH2 near 1572 cm−1 and 1257 cm−1 assigned to the stretching vibrations of N–H and C–N, respectively [35,37], while carbon-carbon peak in aromatics was appeared near 1382 cm−1 [38]. FT-IR graph of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 showed the appearance of characteristic peaks of Ag–AuNCs and UiO-66-NH2 with a weak shift, which further explained that Ag–AuNCs and UiO-66-NH2 self-assembled to form Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images showed that the thickness of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 was estimated at 400 nm which was thicker than the thickness of UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. S3). Next, PXRD pattern in Fig. S4 showed that the peak appearing at 2θ of Ag–AuNCs at 32° was paired to the (111) plane of gold [39]. At the same time, Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 had characteristic diffraction peaks that were basically consistent with pure UiO-66-NH2, indicating that the coating of Ag–AuNCs did not affect the crystal of UiO-66-NH2. In addition, the diffraction peak intensity of UiO-66-NH2 coated with Ag–AuNCs slightly decreased compared to pure UiO-66-NH2. XPS demonstrated that O 1s, Ag 3d, Zr 3d, S 2p and Au 4f peaks were distinguished in Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. 2f). All results indicated the successful formation of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 nanohybrid.

UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectrum were used to investigated the optical properties of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 nanohybrid. UV–vis spectrum in Fig. S5a showed a weak shoulder peak at 400 nm for the AuNCs, and the absorption peak of Ag+ modified AuNCs was not shifted. UiO-66-NH2 showed three absorption peaks at 240 nm, 275 nm and 385 nm, assigning to π-π* transition in BDC-NH2, the LMCT effect between BDC-NH2 and Zr–O clusters, n-π* transition in BDC-NH2, respectively [40,41]. For Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2, the corresponding peak intensities were slightly enhanced, but the characteristic absorption of both constituents could be identified. Fig. S5b showed that UiO-66-NH2 existed a broad emission at 465 nm with weak emission intensity, and Ag–AuNCs had a characteristic emission peak at 650 nm. For Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2, the characteristic emission peaks of UiO-66-NH2 and Ag–AuNCs were found simultaneously, and the emission peak intensity at 650 nm was lower than free Ag–AuNCs, and the fluorescence quantum yield was decreased from 7.92% to 6.92%, the fluorescence lifetime was reduced from 7.98 μs to 7.66 μs, which may be caused by fluorescence resonance energy transfer process (Fig. S6) [42,43]. When DCP was added to the Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 solution, the fluorescence intensity was significantly increased at 465 nm and decreased at 650 nm, indicating a strong response to DCP. Therefore, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor could be constructed to sensitive detect DCP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 for DCP in water
ce:section.ce:para: So far, various fluorescent probes for detecting DCP have been reported in the literature, and most of them relied on small organic fluorescent molecules, which often used the specific interaction between functional groups and DCP to produce fluorescent signals [44–47]. However, these probes have the characteristics of complex synthesis, poor water solubility and cytotoxicity, which caused secondary pollution to the environment after interaction with DCP. At present, there are few reports on the quantitative analysis of DCP using nanozymes, especially MOF materials. Our research group prepared MOF materials with oxidase activity to catalyze the oxidation of O-phenylenediamine (OPD), and used the interaction between DCP and OPD-oxidation product to induce the generation of fluorescence response [48]. However, this catalytic activity led to a competitive mode reaction system that reduced the sensitivity to DCP. Inspired by this, enzyme cascades with signal amplification effects have been incorporated into the detection system of DCP to improve the sensitivity of MOF to DCP.

Firstly, the key factors of Ag–AuNCs concentration, UiO-66-NH2 concentration and reaction time were explored to construct an analytical system for highly sensitive detection of DCP (Figs. S7a–c). The pH study in Fig. S7d showed that the fluorescence intensity ratio (F465 nm/F650 nm) of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 remained consistent at different pH conditions, which proved the stability of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2. Sensing performance of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 for DCP was further evaluated under optimal sensing conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, emission intensity of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 at 465 nm increased positively with DCP concentration. Fig. S8 showed the amplification of fluorescence spectra of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 at 650 nm. It could be clearly seen that emission intensity at 650 nm reduced with DCP concentration, and the color fluorescence picture of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 changed from red to blue. Based on this, F465 nm/F650 nm was linearly analyzed with the concentration of DCP (Fig. 3b), and it was found that a good linear relationship existed between F465 nm/F650 nm and the concentration of DCP (15 μM–180 μM). The linear fitting equation was y = 0.12x+8.10 (R2 = 0.998), detection limit was 0.15 μM, and quantitation limit was 0.50 μM. In addition, F-test was used to analyze the sensing results obtained by fluorescence method and the GC-MS method (Table S1). At different DCP levels, the F-test measurements (FS1 2/S2 2, S is the standard deviation) were all below the critical value of F (19.00) [49], indicating that the fluorescence method and GC-MS method has no significant difference at the confidence level of 95%.

Next, to obtain the selectivity of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 probe, a number of distractors were selected. As shown in Fig. 3c and d, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, Na3PO4 and DCP led a large fluorescence response to Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 at 465 nm, which may be due to the specific binding of the metal center to phosphate group to block the LMCT effect. TEA is alkaline and could hydrolyze UiO-66-NH2 to release BDC-NH2 to generate fluorescent signals. MCE could quench the fluorescence of Ag–AuNCs at 650 nm, which may be due to the combination of sulfhydryl groups in MCE with Ag+ and Au+ [50]. Overall, only DCP could change the fluorescence intensities of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 at 465 nm and 650 nm simultaneously. The anti-interference experiment also proved that Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 had good anti-interference ability to DCP (Fig. S9). Therefore, Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 can be used for specific identification of DCP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible principle of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 for DCP detection
ce:section.ce:para: So far, it has been reported several times that UiO-66-NH2 containing Zr–O clusters shows a high hydrolysis rate for organophosphorus nerve agents and can be used for detoxification of nerve agents by rapidly cutting the P-X bond (Fig. S10) [51]. Thus the possible sensing mechanism of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 for DCP in water was illustrated in Fig. 4 a. After reaction with DCP, the crystal structure of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 was not collapsed, which ruled out the fluorescence enhancement caused by the structure collapse (Fig. S11a). When DCP was introduced, the FT-IR spectra showed that an asymmetric stretching vibration peak of P–O bond appeared at 1038 cm−1, indicating that DEP, the hydrolysate of DCP, was bonded with UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. S11b) [52,53]. This strong interaction would hinder LMCT effect. After introduction of DCP, fluorescence lifetime of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 at 465 nm rised from 11.48 ns to 16.74 ns (Fig. S12a), which further suggested that DCP promoted fluorescence recovery by blocking the LMCT effect inside UiO-66-NH2. Subsequently, we explored the reason for the attenuation of fluorescence at 650 nm. As shown in Fig. 4b, DCP, DEP and Cl− were introduced into Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2, respectively. DEP did no affect the fluorescence of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2. However, Hydrolysate Cl− weakened the emission intensity of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 at 650 nm, indicating that Cl− might be the main reason for the decrease of fluorescence at 650 nm. Fluorescence lifetime of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 at 650 nm did not significantly alter after the introduction of DCP (Fig. S12b), revealing that there was no energy transfer between DCP and Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 [54]. Next, the morphology of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 in the presence or absence of DCP was observed by TEM. Compared with the morphology without DCP (Fig. 4c) and d showed that the surface of Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 was relatively smooth and flat when DCP was presence, and AuNCs changed from a relatively clustered state to a relatively decentralized state. And the fluorescence quantum yield was decreased from 6.92% to 4.24%. All these indicated that Cl− bound to Ag+ in Ag–AuNCs, resulting in the release of Ag+ from AuNCs. Therefore, we speculated that the sensing mechanism between Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 and DCP is that DCP is hydrolyzed by UiO-66-NH2 to produce DEP and Cl−, then DEP is anchored to UiO-66-NH2, hindering the LMCT of UiO-66-NH2 and leading to fluorescence recovery of BDC-NH2. On the other hand, the hydrolysate Cl− bound to Ag, so that Ag+ couldn't trigger AIEE process of AuNCs, resulting in AuNCs fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DCP vapor detection using Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 hydrogel sheet
ce:section.ce:para: In view of the excellent sensing performance of the fluorescent probe for DCP in water, we prepared agarose hydrogel containing Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 for gas phase detection of DCP to investigate the applicability of the probe for field use (Fig. 5 a). Similarly, experimental conditions were optimized to obtain high sensitivity. Finally, optimal dosage of Ag–AuNCs and UiO-66-NH2 were selected as 250 μL mL−1 and 150 μg mL−1, and reaction time was 1.5 min (Fig. S13). Fig. 5b showed that when the hydrogel sheets were exposed to DCP vapor from 0.001 to 132 ppm, fluorescent color of composite hydrogels gradually altered from purplish red to sky blue under a 254 nm UV lamp. When DCP vapor was as low as 0.004 ppm, the fluorescent color variation could still be identified by the naked eye. RGB values of fluorescent color pictures of hydrogel sheets were calculated by Image J. It was found that the composite hydrogel showed a gradient response to DCP vapor (Fig. 5c), and showed a good linear relationship in the range of 0–0.01 ppm (Fig. 5d, Fig. S14), with R2 = 0.982. Based on these results, a LOD of 1.02 ppb was calculated, which is lower than the LOD of most previously reported sensors (Table S2), which proves the high sensitivity of the composite hydrogel to DCP. At the same time, the LOD of composite hydrogel was lower than the reported concentration of sarin that was immediately life-threatening or health threatening (7 ppb), and considering the higher reactivity of sarin, the composite hydrogel could achieve visual detection of a low concentration of actual sarin vapor [55].

In addition, to determine the specific recognition ability of agarose hydrogel loaded with Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 for DCP vapor, some organophosphorus compounds and acid vapor such as TMP, TEP, TEPi, DMMP, DCNP, THF, HCl, and ethanol were selected for the interference experiments. As illustrated in Fig. 5e, the fluorescent color of hydrogel sheets altered from purple red to sky blue after exposure to DCP vapor, while the fluorescent color remained unchanged when exposed to other vapors (Fig. S15). These results all indicated that the agarose hydrogel loaded with Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 had excellent selectivity for DCP vapor.

In conclusion, Ag–AuNCs self-assemble with UiO-66-NH2 through electrostatic interaction to generate Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 nanohybrid, which built a ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform based on nanozyme cascade for ultra-sensitive detection of DCP. The core UiO-66-NH2 showed faint blue fluorescence because of LMCT effect, and the introduction of DCP occupied the Zr–O node of UiO-66-NH2, which blocked LMCT effect and promoted the significant fluorescence enhancement of UiO-66-NH2. Meanwhile, UiO-66-NH2 was used for the catalytic degradation of nerve agents, Cl−, the hydrolysate of DCP, was ingeniously combined with Ag+ in the outer Ag–AuNCs, which hindered the AIEE process of AuNCs triggered by Ag+ and leaded to reduced luminescence. More significantly, we prepared the Ag–AuNCs@UiO-66-NH2 loaded agarose hydrogel, which changed the fluorescence color from purplish red to sky blue when exposed to DCP vapor for easy identification by the naked eye. By integrating the agarose hydrogel-based detection system with a smartphone, the quantitative assessment of DCP vapor was realized, which reduced the detection cost and complexity and offered a new idea for the on-site monitoring of nerve agent gas.

You Lei: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Nan Gao: Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Pengcheng Huang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Fang-Ying Wu: Data curation, Methodology, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (No.22164013 and No.21864018).
